title.zh: 與鬼魂共存：過去的物理痕跡如何形塑唐人街的文化創傷
title.en: Living with Ghosts: How Physical Traces of the Past Shape Cultural Trauma in Chinatowns

summary.zh: 【總結】本研究突破了文化創傷理論中現實主義與建構主義的二元對立，提出「物質主義」的分析框架，揭示唐人街作為「文化儲蓄庫」如何累積過去的傷害痕跡，成為「創傷圖標」的集合。研究發現，唐人街的保存運動源於對過去反華迫害的集體記憶，而物理環境中的「固執圖標」、「熵減圖標」和「修復圖標」三種類型，將抽象的歷史傷害轉化為具體可感的空間體驗，使不同世代的亞裔社群能跨越時間與分歧，建立情感連結。這項研究不僅解釋了唐人街保存運動的動力來源，更開創了文化創傷研究的新視角。【研究問題】文章探討的核心問題是：過去的傷害事件如何與當代世代產生共鳴？傳統文化創傷理論存在現實主義（強調歷史事件的直接影響）與建構主義（強調集體記憶的後續生產）之間的爭議，前者忽視了記憶的社會建構過程，後者則缺乏對實際經驗的關注。此外，社會學界對唐人街的研究多集中在移民模式或隔離區理論，卻未能解釋為何在邊緣化趨勢下，唐人街對亞裔社群仍具有深遠意義。【研究方法】研究團隊採用混合研究方法，對北美15個城市的83份唐人街規劃文件進行內容分析，時間跨度從1970年至2024年。此外，研究人員在卡爾加里、芝加哥、愛德蒙頓、費城和多倫多等城市進行了68次半結構化訪談，對象包括社區活動家、社團領袖、企業家、藝術家和城市規劃師等「地景塑造者」。訪談語言包括英語、普通話和粵語，參與者年齡介於18至90歲，性別和出生地分佈均勻。研究過程中，研究團隊還參與了多個社區會議和地景塑造活動，以三角驗證訪談數據。【結果】研究發現，創傷敘事在唐人街保存運動中具有普遍性，超過80%的受訪者在不同程度上將唐人街的歷史與過去的反華迫害聯繫起來。研究識別出三種關鍵的「創傷圖標」類型：「固執圖標」（如卡爾加里的Harry Hays聯邦大樓）象徵過去的傷害仍持續存在；「熵減圖標」（如費城的已關閉中國社區文化中心）因衰敗或消失而成為象徵；「修復圖標」（如紀念碑）則是社區將傷害痕跡轉化為紀念物的嘗試。這些圖標共同構成唐人街作為「文化儲蓄庫」的獨特性，使不同世代的亞裔社群能透過具體的物理空間體驗，產生跨越時間的集體認同感和團結。研究顯示，創傷敘事能有效弥合族群和世代分歧，成為唐人街保存運動的核心動力。
summary.en: **Summary:** This research breaks through the realist/constructivist divide in cultural trauma theory by proposing a materialist framework that examines how Chinatowns function as 'cultural reservoirs' accumulating traces of past harms. The study identifies three types of 'trauma icons'—'stubborn' (persistent buildings like Harry Hays federal building), 'entropic' (objects that gain meaning through deterioration), and 'remedial' (memorials created by affected communities)—that transform abstract historical trauma into tangible spatial experiences. These icons enable Asian American/Canadian communities across generations to build emotional connections despite temporal and social divisions. This research not only explains the motivation behind Chinatown preservation movements but pioneers a new perspective in cultural trauma studies by emphasizing the material mediation between past events and contemporary memory.**Problem:** The article addresses how harmful past events resonate with subsequent generations who didn't experience them directly. Cultural trauma theory has been polarized between realists (focusing on direct effects of historical events) and constructivists (emphasizing the social construction of memory), with the former neglecting how trauma is represented and the latter undertheorizing the connection between actual harm and collective memory. Additionally, sociological research on Chinatowns has primarily focused on immigration patterns or enclave theories, failing to explain why these neighborhoods remain significant to Asian American/Canadian communities despite their marginalization in contemporary migration processes.**Approach:** The researchers employed a mixed-methods approach, analyzing 83 planning documents from 15 North American cities spanning 1970-2024. They conducted 68 semi-structured interviews with 'placemakers' (community activists, leaders, business owners, artists, and urban planners) in cities including Calgary, Chicago, Edmonton, Philadelphia, and Toronto. Interviews were conducted in English, Mandarin, and Cantonese with participants aged 18-90. The research team also participated in community meetings and placemaking initiatives to triangulate interview findings with direct observation, using 'neighborhood narrative frames' as an analytical tool to identify how placemakers conceptualized Chinatown identity and preservation motivations.**Results:** The study found that trauma narratives are ubiquitous in Chinatown preservation efforts, with over 80% of participants connecting Chinatown's history to past anti-Chinese discrimination in some way. The researchers identified three key 'trauma icon' types: 'stubborn icons' (permanent structures like Calgary's Harry Hays building that symbolize ongoing harm), 'entropic icons' (objects that gain significance through deterioration, like Philadelphia's former Chinese Community & Cultural Center), and 'remedial icons' (community-created memorials). These icons collectively constitute Chinatowns as 'cultural reservoirs' that create a coherence of past harm greater than the sum of their parts. Critically, trauma narratives effectively bridged ethnic and generational divisions, becoming the primary motivation for Chinatown preservation movements across diverse communities.

keyArticle.title: Living with Ghosts: How Physical Traces of the Past Shape Cultural Trauma in Chinatowns
